title: Atiku Abubakar & PDP v. INEC & Tinubu — Presidential Election Petition
issue: PDP and its candidate Atiku Abubakar challenged Tinubu's election victory, alleging certificate forgery, dual citizenship, and widespread electoral malpractice. The petition traversed Tinubu's Chicago State University certificate and his 1993 US drug forfeiture.
outcome: The Supreme Court upheld Tinubu's election unanimously on 26 October 2023, finding that petitioners did not prove their allegations to the required standard. The court did not rule on the certificate question on its merits. Critics argue the judgment did not address the substantive factual disputes.

title: SERAP v. FGN — Disclosure of constituency project allocations
issue: SERAP sued the federal government under the Freedom of Information Act demanding full disclosure of how ₦96 billion in National Assembly constituency project funds were allocated and spent. The FG argued disclosure would prejudice national interest.
outcome: The court ruled in SERAP's favour, ordering full disclosure within 30 days. The government did not comply within the deadline. SERAP filed a contempt motion. As of 2026 the disclosure had not been made — a pattern of ignoring court-ordered FOIA compliance.

title: NLC & TUC v. FGN — Subsidy removal and minimum wage dispute
issue: The Nigeria Labour Congress and TUC challenged the adequacy of the government's response to the hardship caused by the subsidy removal, and demanded a new national minimum wage of ₦494,000/month. They threatened and began a general strike in July 2024.
outcome: The National Industrial Court issued an order restraining the strike. The government and labour reached a negotiated settlement — a new minimum wage of ₦70,000/month was agreed in July 2024 (below labour's demand but above the previous ₦30,000). Implementation at state level was uneven.

title: SERAP v. FGN — Recovery and disclosure of looted COVID-19 funds
issue: SERAP sued for disclosure of how COVID-19 intervention funds disbursed by the PTF (Presidential Task Force) were spent, and demanded recovery of misappropriated sums. Multiple agencies — NDDC, NCDC, state governments — had been implicated in diversions.
outcome: The court ordered the government to disclose details of COVID-19 fund expenditure and recovery efforts. No meaningful public disclosure was made. SERAP flagged non-compliance to the court. This case typifies a recurring pattern: government loses FOIA-related cases and ignores compliance.

title: Governor Fubara v. FGN — Rivers State State of Emergency challenge
issue: Governor Siminalayi Fubara challenged the constitutionality of Tinubu's declaration of a State of Emergency in Rivers State on 18 March 2025, arguing that the political crisis did not meet the constitutional threshold for such a declaration and that it was politically motivated.
outcome: The Supreme Court upheld the declaration in a split decision, finding that the President acted within his constitutional powers. Fubara was restored to office when the six-month SoE expired. Critics viewed the ruling as setting a dangerous precedent for the use of emergency powers against political opponents.

title: Incorporated Trustees of Socio-Economic Rights v. AGF — FOIA blanket refusal
issue: Civil society groups challenged a pattern of blanket FOIA refusals by federal MDAs, arguing that agencies were systematically denying access to public information without valid exemption grounds as required by the FOI Act 2011.
outcome: The court found in favour of the plaintiffs and ordered specific MDAs to respond to pending FOIA requests. Compliance was partial — some agencies released documents, others continued to delay. The Attorney General's office did not issue a circular mandating compliance across all MDAs.

title: Binance / Tigran Gambaryan detention — US diplomatic and legal pressure
court: Federal High Court + US Congressional pressure
issue: Binance executive Tigran Gambaryan, a US citizen, was detained in Nigeria from February 2024 on charges related to Binance's alleged facilitation of naira manipulation and tax evasion. His detention without trial for months drew condemnation from the US Congress, which threatened sanctions.
outcome: Gambaryan was released in October 2024 after diplomatic intervention and intense US congressional pressure. Binance paid a $35 million fine and entered a compliance agreement with the Nigerian government. The case raised serious concerns about due process, arbitrary detention, and the use of detained foreigners as diplomatic leverage.

title: SERAP v. Senate — Disclosure of Senators' running costs
issue: SERAP sued the National Assembly demanding disclosure of how 'running cost' allowances — estimated at ₦21 million per senator per quarter — are spent. The NASS argued that such disclosure was not required under the FOI Act as they were legislative expenditures.
outcome: The court ruled that the National Assembly is subject to the FOI Act and ordered disclosure. The Senate did not comply. This case is emblematic of the broader failure to enforce transparency obligations against the legislature. No contempt proceedings have succeeded.

title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Governors — LGA financial autonomy
issue: The Tinubu administration filed a suit at the Supreme Court seeking to enforce direct payment of federal allocations to local government councils, bypassing state governments that had been withholding LGA funds. 35 state governors were respondents.
outcome: The Supreme Court ruled unanimously in favour of the FGN in July 2024, ordering that LGA funds be paid directly from FAAC to LGA accounts. This was one of the administration's significant legal wins. However, governors found indirect ways to recapture funds through LGA chairmen they controlled. Implementation remains partial.

title: Journalists arrested during #EndBadGovernance protests — press freedom cases
issue: During and after the August 2024 #EndBadGovernance protests, security agencies arrested dozens of protesters and several journalists. Some were charged with treasonable felony. Courts in multiple states ordered the release of protesters held without charge, finding the detentions unlawful.
outcome: Courts ordered the release of unlawfully detained protesters in several instances. Some detainees were released; others faced prolonged prosecution on serious charges. A few journalists had charges dropped after domestic and international press freedom advocacy. Several cases remained ongoing in 2026.
